contentHtml:
Releasing Shame Stored in the Hips

Sue Johnson, the architect of Emotionally Focused Therapy, has spent a lifetime mapping the intricate dance of human connection. She teaches that we are wired for bonding, for the deep, resonant safety of being truly seen by another. But what happens when the very ground of that connection — our own body — becomes a landscape of shame, a place we can no longer inhabit with ease?

For so many, the body—particularly the deep, primal bowl of the hips—becomes a silent repository for everything we’ve been taught to suppress. It’s the shame of being too much or not enough, too sensitive or too closed, too sexual or not sexual enough. This shame doesn’t just live in the mind; it embeds itself in our tissues, tightening the fascia and creating a frozen landscape in the very center of our creative power.

Releasing this stored shame is not a matter of positive thinking or intellectual understanding. It is a somatic journey, a pilgrimage back into the body’s wisdom. It requires us to meet ourselves with a fierce and unwavering tenderness, to breathe into the frozen places, and to allow the stored energy to finally, finally move. This isn't just a shift. This is a burn. It's a ritual.

The Body as a Living Archive

The idea that the body stores emotion is the bedrock of classical Tantra and somatic psychology alike. Your body is a living archive of your entire life experience. Every joy, sorrow, connection, and rupture is recorded not just in your brain, but in the fabric of your musculature and cellular memory. The hips, with their deep sockets and powerful muscles, are a place where the unsaid and unfelt often come to rest.

When we feel unsafe, we instinctively curl inward. The hip flexors are central to this protective clenching. When a child is shamed for their exuberance or emotional expression, they learn to unconsciously tighten this core part of themselves. Over decades, this chronic tension becomes somatic armor, a way of containing overwhelming emotions. But this containment disconnects us from our vitality and pleasure. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen this disconnection lead to chronic pain, sexual dysfunction, and a pervasive sense of being alienated from one’s own life force.

You do not need to earn the right to be held.

To begin the process of release, we must first acknowledge this reality. We must approach our bodies not as objects to be fixed, but as sacred texts to be read with reverence and compassion. Your pain is not a pathology; it is a messenger. The tension in your hips is not a flaw; it is a story waiting to be heard. The journey begins with listening, with turning your awareness inward and asking, with genuine curiosity, “What is being held here?”

The Energetics of Shame

Shame is a physiological state. It’s the experience of being seen in a moment of perceived unworthiness, and it triggers a profound response in the autonomic nervous system: the head drops, the shoulders round, the gaze averts. This is the dorsal vagal response, a state of freeze or collapse—the body’s most primitive survival strategy.

When shame becomes chronic, when it is woven into the fabric of our early life experiences, this collapse state can become a baseline. We learn to live in a state of subtle, perpetual shutdown. The energy that should be flowing freely through the body, particularly through the lower chakras associated with grounding, creativity, and life force, becomes stagnant. The hips, which correspond to the second chakra, Svadhisthana, are the seat of our emotions, our desires, and our creative power. When this center is blocked by the frozen energy of shame, we lose access to these vital aspects of ourselves.

This is about understanding the mechanics of our own liberation. The energy of shame is dense and cold. To release it, we must bring in warmth, light, and movement. This is the work of embodiment: inhabiting the body so fully that the light of our consciousness can thaw the frozen places, breathing warmth into the tissues, and using intuitive movement to unwind tension. The body is a sacred text, and learning its language is the first step toward healing.

Vulnerability is not a strategy. It is a surrender.

The Path of Somatic Release: Practices for Thawing the Hips

Releasing shame from the body is not a forceful act. You cannot command the tissues to let go. It is a process of creating the conditions for release, offering the body the safety it needs to unwind on its own time. This is the essence of somatic practice: less doing, more allowing.

Begin by creating a space where you feel safe and unobserved. This is not about performance; it is about intimacy with yourself. Lie on your back with your knees bent and your feet flat on the floor. Place your hands on your lower belly, just below the navel. For a few moments, simply notice the gentle rise and fall of your breath beneath your hands. This is your anchor, your return to the present moment. From this place of grounded awareness, you can begin to explore.

A profound practice is intuitive movement. Allow your knees to gently fall open to the sides and then slowly bring them back together. This is a conversation with your body. Bring your full attention to the sensations in your hips, pelvis, and lower back. Notice any tightness or resistance without judgment. Meet these sensations with your breath, imagining it as a warm, golden light bringing the message, “You are safe. You are allowed to let go.”

The supported bridge pose is also powerful. With knees bent and feet on the floor, lift your hips and slide a block under your sacrum. Allow the weight of your pelvis to rest on the support. This gentle inversion can create a profound release in the hip flexors. Return to your breath, allowing it to move deep into the pelvis. You might experience physical or emotional release. You're here to become the silence that transforms the room. Allow it all to be present. This is somatic alchemy, transforming stored trauma into embodied presence.

What you can feel, you can heal.

The Radical Act of Pleasure

Shame and pleasure cannot coexist. Shame constricts, pleasure expands. Shame numbs, pleasure awakens. One of the most radical and transformative paths to releasing shame stored in the hips is the intentional cultivation of pleasure. This is not about performative sexuality or chasing sensation for its own sake. It is about reclaiming your birthright to feel good in your own skin. It is about re-sensitizing the tissues of the body to the subtle currents of life force energy, or prana, that are always available to us.

This journey begins with sensuality, engaging the senses to be present. Take a warm bath with essential oils. Feel the water on your skin, notice the scent, listen to the sound. This is not an indulgence; it is somatic nourishment, communicating to your body that it is worthy of care and pleasure. As you cultivate sensual presence, you can bring it into your relationship with your own body.

Explore self-touch with a new quality of attention. Touch your body as a lover. Use oil to slowly massage your feet, legs, belly, and hips. Notice the sensations and the stories your mind tells. Let go of the stories and be with the sensations. Allow yourself to feel the pleasure of your own touch. This is a profound practice of self-love, a direct antidote to shame. A client once described this as meeting her own body for the first time. The work of researchers like Sue Johnson shows that secure attachment is built on this kind of responsive connection. When we offer it to ourselves, we rewire our relational blueprint.

Integrating the Shadow: The Fierce Grace of Release

As you create safety and cultivate pleasure, the stored energy of shame will surface. This phase can be messy and intense. You may experience waves of emotion, vivid dreams, or a powerful urge to scream, cry, or shake. This is not a sign that you are doing something wrong; it is a sign the process is working. The frozen energy is thawing and needs to move.

What you are feeling is not too much. It is exactly right.

It is essential to have practices to navigate this phase. Shaking or tremoring is one of the body’s most natural ways to discharge excess energy. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and allow your body to shake. Let the movement be involuntary, arising from the inside out. Allow any sounds to be made. This is about getting free, a primal process that bypasses the conscious mind.

Working with the emotional content that arises is also essential. Journaling is a powerful tool for externalizing the stories and emotions held in the body. Write without censorship. You might also find it helpful to work with a skilled somatic therapist who can provide a safe container for your process. A compassionate witness can be incredibly healing, especially if your original shame wounds were relational. Remember, you are not broken. You are a human being who has stored pain in your body, and you are now, with immense courage, learning to set yourself free. The practice of embodied presence is a lifelong journey.

The Dance of Relationship: Shame in Connection

The journey of releasing shame is both personal and relational. Our deepest shame wounds often occur in relationship, and it is in relationship that they can be most powerfully healed. Stored shame in the hips impacts how we move, feel, and connect, creating a barrier to intimacy and a fear of being seen.

Healing in partnership requires a shared commitment to creating a space of radical safety. It means communicating our inner experience with honesty. It might mean saying, “I’m feeling a lot of tension in my body right now.” This vulnerable communication is a powerful act of love, inviting your partner into your inner world and transforming the bedroom from a place of pressure into a sanctuary for healing.

You are not stuck. You are choosing.

For the partner, the role is not to fix, but to witness and hold. It is to offer a steady, loving presence that communicates, “I am here with you. I am not afraid of your feelings.” It is to touch with reverence and listen with an open heart. This is the sacred dance of conscious intimacy, co-creating a relational field where both partners feel safe. In this sweet and lovely moment, I open my heart to a new day, a new beginning, a new being and a new reality. I embrace the eternal flame of love for all time. It is a journey from isolation to connection. Breath as the bridge between bodies can support this journey.

The Long Road Home: Integrating a New Embodiment

Releasing shame from the hips is not a one-time event. It is a process of returning, again and again, to the body and the present moment. It is a gradual re-patterning of the nervous system. There will be days you feel open and free, and days when old patterns return. This is the natural rhythm of healing. The goal is not a permanent state of release, but the capacity to meet whatever arises with kindness.

As you continue, you will notice subtle shifts. You may feel more grounded and centered. You may discover new creative energy and a desire for authentic expression. You may experience a deepening of your capacity for pleasure and intimacy. This is the fruit of the journey: a felt sense of wholeness and trust in your body’s wisdom.

This is the long road home to yourself, a journey worthy of your deepest devotion. It is the sacred work of reclaiming your body as a temple. May you remember, on the hard days, that you are the embodiment of infinite love. Every breath and every moment of conscious presence is a step on that path. The healing power of surrender is a key ally.

This site provides educational content about conscious intimacy and sacred sexuality. It is not therapy. For sexual trauma, consult a licensed somatic or sex therapist.

Continue reading: your body as sacred ground.
faqHtml:
Frequently Asked Questions

Q: How do I know if I’m storing shame in my hips?

A: Common signs include chronic tightness or pain in the hips, lower back, or pelvic area; a limited range of motion in the hips; a feeling of being disconnected from your lower body or your sexuality; and a tendency to clench your glutes or hip flexors when you feel stressed or anxious. However, the most reliable way to know is to begin to cultivate a practice of inner listening. Lie down, bring your attention to your hips, and ask, “What is here?” The answer may come in the form of physical sensation, emotion, memory, or imagery. Trust what you discover.

Q: Can I do this work on my own, or do I need a therapist?

A: It is possible to make significant progress on your own through the kinds of somatic practices described in this article. However, if you have a history of significant trauma, or if you find that the process of release is overwhelming, it is highly recommended that you seek the support of a qualified somatic therapist. A skilled therapist can provide a safe container for your experience and help you navigate the more challenging aspects of the journey.

Q: How long does it take to release stored shame?

A: There is no timeline for healing. The process is unique to each individual and depends on many factors, including the nature of your history, your current life circumstances, and the consistency of your practice. The goal is not to get to a destination, but to learn to be with yourself in a new way. It is a lifelong journey of coming home to your body. Be patient and compassionate with yourself. The body releases on its own schedule, not the mind’s.

Q: What if I don’t feel anything when I do these practices?

A: Numbness is a very common form of somatic armor. If you don’t feel much at first, don’t be discouraged. This is simply information. It is the body’s way of protecting itself from overwhelming sensation. Continue to meet yourself with gentle, consistent presence. Bring your breath into the numb areas. Touch them with kindness. Over time, as the body learns that it is safe to feel, the numbness will begin to thaw and sensation will return.

Q: Can this work really improve my sex life?

A: Absolutely. Shame is one of the biggest inhibitors of sexual pleasure and connection. When we are holding shame in the body, it is very difficult to fully surrender to the experience of pleasure. Releasing this stored shame can lead to a profound awakening of your sexual energy, a greater capacity for orgasm, and a deeper sense of intimacy and connection with your partner. It is a journey from performative sex to embodied, soulful lovemaking.
